perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to keep confidential this Agreement and not to distribute or show copies of it or disclose the contents hereof to any person or entity not subject to this Agreement (except its Legal and accounting advisors with a written agreement to keep the seine confidential) without the prior written consent of the other Patty, except to the extent required by applicable law or judicial or administrative process.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to keep confidential this Agreement and not to distribute or show copies of it or disclose the contents hereof to any person or entity not subject to this Agreement (except its Legal and accounting advisors) without the prior written consent of the other Patty.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'except to the extent required by applicable law or judicial or administrative process' creates a legal contradiction. This phrase is a standard exception that allows disclosure when legally mandated. Omitting it implies that a party must maintain confidentiality even when compelled by law, which is not legally enforceable and could put a party in legal jeopardy for contempt of court.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could prevent a party from disclosing information protected by attorney-client privilege if compelled by judicial process, directly contradicting Florida Statute § 90.502, which acknowledges this privilege and outlines exceptions where disclosure is required or permitted.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 90 Section 502 - 2024 Florida Statutes Home Bills Calendar Committees Senators Statutes Contact Us Directions & Parking Help Menu 2024 Florida Statutes < Back to Statute Search Title VII EVIDENCE Chapter 90 EVIDENCE CODE SECTION 502 Lawyer-client privilege. 90.502 Lawyer-client privilege. — (1) For purposes of this section: (a) A “lawyer” is a person authorized, or reasonably believed by the client to be authorized, to practice law in any state or nation. (b) A “client” is any person, public officer, corporation, association, or other organization or entity, either public or private, who consults a lawyer with the purpose of obtaining legal services or who is rendered legal services by a lawyer. (c) A communication between lawyer and client is “confidential” if it is not intended to be disclosed to third persons other than: 1. Those to whom disclosure is in furtherance of the rendition of legal services to the client. 2. Those reasonably necessary for the transmission of the communication. (2) A client has a privilege to refuse to disclose, and to prevent any other person from disclosing, the contents of confidential communications when such other person learned of the communications because they were made in the rendition of legal services to the client. (3) The privilege may be claimed by: (a) The client. (b) A guardian or conservator of the client. (c) The personal representative of a deceased client. (d) A successor, assignee, trustee in dissolution, or any similar representative of an organization, corporation, or association or other entity, either public or private, whether or not in existence. (e) The lawyer, but only on behalf of the client. The lawyer’s authority to claim the privilege is presumed in the absence of contrary evidence. (4) There is no lawyer-client privilege under this section when: (a) The services of the lawyer were sought or obtained to enable or aid anyone to commit or plan to commit what the client knew was a crime or fraud. (b) A communication is relevant to an issue between parties who claim through the same deceased client. (c) A communication is relevant to an issue of breach of duty by the lawyer to the client or by the client to the lawyer, arising from the lawyer-client relationship. (d)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes an exception allowing disclosure 'to the extent required by applicable law or judicial or administrative process.' The changed text removes this exception, creating a scenario where a party might be contractually obligated to maintain confidentiality even when legally compelled to disclose information. This directly contradicts the general principle that legal obligations supersede contractual ones. While the provided Florida Statute § 90.502 focuses on lawyer-client privilege, the broader implication is that legal privileges and mandates for disclosure exist and must be respected. By removing the 'except to the extent required by applicable law' clause, the modified contract creates a situation where a party could be in breach of contract for complying with a legal order, which is an untenable legal position.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party therefore consents to the entry of a restraining order and a preliminary and permanent injunction restraining any such violation without proof of actual damages. Each Party agrees that any money damages shall include all pecuniary beets obtained by the receiving Party and its Affiliates as a result of any brunch of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party therefore consents to the entry of a restraining order and a preliminary and permanent injunction restraining any such violation. Each Party agrees that any money damages shall include all pecuniary beets obtained by the receiving Party and its Affiliates as a result of any brunch of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'without proof of actual damages' introduces a legal contradiction. Florida law generally requires proof of actual damages to obtain monetary relief in breach of contract cases.  Consenting to an injunction without requiring proof of actual damages could be argued as waiving certain legal protections, but the main issue is eliminating the need to prove damages for monetary claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 672.719(1)(b) - Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement of proving damages, the contract attempts to bypass standard legal procedures required for obtaining monetary relief in breach of contract cases.  While parties can limit remedies, this removal creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability under Florida law regarding proving damages for breach of contract as outlined by Florida Statute § 672.719(1)(b).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'without proof of actual damages,' which creates a contradiction because Florida law typically requires proof of actual damages for monetary relief in breach of contract cases. The changed text aims to bypass standard legal procedures required for obtaining monetary relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically § 2-719.(1)(a), indicates that parties can limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable. However, the Perturbation Explanation highlights that the removal of the 'proof of actual damages' requirement creates a potential issue with enforceability under Florida law. While parties can modify remedies, removing a standard requirement creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Therefore the omission creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Florida without giving effect to the principles of conflict of law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Florida.
perturbation.explanation: Omitting 'without giving effect to the principles of conflict of law' means that normal conflict of law principles would apply. These principles determine which jurisdiction's laws apply when there is a dispute involving parties from different jurisdictions. By omitting this, there is no instruction on conflict of law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 671.301(1) - Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 671.301(1) allows parties to choose the applicable law, but in the absence of an explicit choice that considers conflict of law principles, the court will apply its own conflict of law rules to determine the governing law. The omission creates ambiguity regarding the parties' intent and may lead to a court applying conflict of law principles, which contradicts the original clause's intent to avoid them.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 301 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. F.S. 671.301 671.301 Effective date; provision for transition; preservation of old transition provision. — (1) This act shall take effect at 12:01 a.m. on January 1, 1980. (2) Transactions validly entered into after January 1, 1967, and before the effective date of this act, and which were subject to the provisions of chapter 65-254, Laws of Florida, as amended, and which would be subject to this act as amended if they had been entered into after the effective date of this act and the rights, duties, and interests flowing from such transactions remain valid after the latter date and may be terminated, completed, consummated, or enforced as required or permitted by this act. Security interests arising out of such transactions which are perfected when this act becomes effective shall remain perfected until they lapse as provided in this act and may be continued as permitted by this act, except as stated in s. 680.108 , Florida Statutes 1979. (3) The provisions of s. 680.101 , Florida Statutes 1977, shall continue to apply to this act and for this purpose the two statutes shall be considered one continuous statute. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 1, ch. 69-218; s. 36, ch. 79-398; s. 6, ch. 90-278. Note. — Former s. 680.101.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the laws of Florida will govern the agreement without considering conflict of law principles. The changed text omits this clause, which means conflict of law principles may now be considered. The LLM explanation states that Florida Statute § 671.301(1) allows parties to choose the applicable law, but without specifying to avoid conflict of law, the court will apply its own conflict of law rules. The scraped snippet 1 shows the statute number. The perturbation explanation clarifies that omitting 'without giving effect to the principles of conflict of law' introduces normal conflict of law principles, which contradict the original intent. Therefore, the omission creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any court shall determine that the length of time or the areas coveted by any one individual stipulation, term, or provision excessive, the Agreement shall nut be rendered invalid thereby and the court shall modify the length of time or the area covered to the extent necessary to make the Agreement valid and binding.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any court shall determine that the length of time or the areas coveted by any one individual stipulation, term, or provision excessive, the Agreement shall nut be rendered invalid thereby.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating 'the court shall modify the length of time or the area covered to the extent necessary to make the Agreement valid and binding' eliminates the instruction for judicial reformation. This creates a legal contradiction because Florida courts have the authority to reform contracts, especially non-compete agreements, to make them reasonable and enforceable. Removing this encourages invalidation of the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 542.335(1)(c) - Restraints upon professionals and others
perturbation.law_explanation: This change impacts the enforceability of the non-compete aspects of the agreement. Florida Statute § 542.335(1)(c) specifically allows courts to modify unreasonable restraints rather than invalidate them completely. Omitting the provision for judicial modification contradicts this statutory framework, making it more likely that an overbroad restriction would be struck down entirely, rather than reformed as intended by the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a provision that a court should modify an excessive stipulation to make the agreement valid and binding. The changed text removes this provision, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that Florida courts have the authority to reform contracts, especially non-compete agreements, to make them reasonable and enforceable, and that removing this provision encourages invalidation of the entire agreement. The Law Explanation supports this by citing Florida Statute § 542.335(1)(c), which allows courts to modify unreasonable restraints rather than invalidate them completely. While scraped snippet 2 is irrelevant, and snippet 1 is missing, the removal of the judicial modification clause directly contradicts the described statutory framework allowing for modification, thus creating a legal contradiction. The omission makes it more likely that an overbroad restriction would be struck down entirely, rather than reformed, as intended by the statute.
